The key insight is that the intermediate state — the blackboard — is not just an implementation detail. It is the primary artifact of the system, more valuable than the final output it produces.

This is not an incremental improvement to existing approaches. It is a paradigm shift: from stateless inference to stateful reasoning. Current AI systems treat every interaction as an isolated event — the model reasons, produces output, and forgets. Stateful swarms break this cycle by building persistent, typed, provenance-tracked analytical state that survives across sessions, accumulates over time, and makes every subsequent interaction cheaper and more accurate than the last.

The `supports` field creates an explicit evidence chain: this analysis entry is grounded in three earlier observations, each of which carries its own source provenance. A reviewer can follow the chain from conclusion to evidence to source document.

This is what statefulness means in practice. In a stateless system, all 2,400 entries — the observations, analyses, calculations, gaps, and strategies built over 12 iterations of careful document reading — would be discarded after generating the final output. The next question about the same credit agreement would start from zero: re-reading the same documents, re-extracting the same financial terms, re-discovering the same deviations. In a stateful swarm, this entire analytical state persists. The next question costs a fraction of the first because the expensive understanding has already been built and is immediately queryable.

input.old_string:
## 9. Conclusion

We have presented a swarm-based document analysis architecture that achieves competitive benchmark performance at dramatically lower cost than published alternatives. The system's primary contribution is not raw performance but three structural properties: full auditability of the reasoning process through structured blackboard state, predictable cost through model routing and targeted processing, and a natural foundation for persistent cross-session learning.

The memory problem in current agentic systems — context compaction destroying information, session boundaries erasing understanding, RAG retrieving text but not analysis — is not a minor inconvenience. It is the reason that AI-assisted professional work remains fragile and expensive. Systems that forget what they learned yesterday will always pay the full cost of understanding today. Structured persistent state offers a path out of this cycle: pay the cost of understanding once, then build on that understanding incrementally.

The complete system is open-source under the MIT license — use it however you want, including commercially. The repository includes source code, the full benchmark manifest, five annotated example tasks with complete reasoning traces, and verification instructions. Benchmark outputs for all 1,251 tasks are available for independent scoring.

We believe that stateful multi-agent coordination represents an important direction for professional AI systems. We invite collaborators interested in extending this work to additional benchmarks, new problem domains, or production deployment.
input.new_string:
## 9. Beyond Legal: The Stateful Swarm Paradigm Is Domain-Agnostic

The system was validated on a legal benchmark, but the underlying paradigm — task decomposition, persistent blackboard state-building, multi-agent coordination with typed provenance — is not legal-specific. Any domain where professionals build understanding over time through repeated analysis of complex documents is a domain where stateful swarms outperform stateless approaches: financial due diligence, regulatory compliance, medical research synthesis, insurance underwriting, patent analysis, investigative journalism.

The architecture requires no domain-specific training, no custom embeddings, and no fine-tuning. The entire system operates through prompt engineering and coordination logic over standard API calls. Adapting to a new domain requires only a benchmark adapter — the swarm coordination, blackboard management, and state persistence machinery is domain-independent.

## 10. Conclusion

We have presented a stateful swarm architecture for document analysis that achieves competitive benchmark performance at dramatically lower cost than published alternatives — using some of the cheapest models available. The central empirical finding is that the performance comes from the architecture, not from model intelligence: Gemini models that achieve 0% strict all-pass in other agentic systems achieve 17.75% when coordinated through a stateful swarm. This demonstrates that how models are coordinated and how their outputs are structured, accumulated, and reconciled matters more than the capability of any individual model.

The system's primary contribution is not raw performance but a paradigm shift from stateless inference to stateful reasoning, supported by three structural properties: full auditability of the reasoning process through structured blackboard state, predictable cost through model routing and targeted processing, and a natural foundation for persistent cross-session learning that reduces marginal cost by orders of magnitude over time.

The memory problem in current agentic systems — context compaction destroying information, session boundaries erasing understanding, RAG retrieving text but not analysis — is not a minor inconvenience. It is the reason that AI-assisted professional work remains fragile and expensive. Systems that forget what they learned yesterday will always pay the full cost of understanding today. Structured persistent state offers a path out of this cycle: pay the cost of understanding once, then build on that understanding incrementally.

We deliberately open-source with cheap models as the default to make the system genuinely accessible. The barrier to entry is a Gemini API key and $1.30 per task — not an enterprise contract or a frontier model subscription. The complete system is open-source under the MIT license — use it however you want, including commercially. The repository includes source code, the full benchmark manifest, five annotated example tasks with complete reasoning traces, and verification instructions. Benchmark outputs for all 1,251 tasks are available for independent scoring.

We believe that stateful multi-agent coordination represents an important direction for professional AI systems — not because agents are inherently better than monolithic models, but because the structured intermediate state they produce is essential for accountability, cost control, and continuous improvement. We invite collaborators interested in extending this work to additional benchmarks, new problem domains, or production deployment.

content.text:
Done. The whitepaper now has all the key insights from the README:

1. **"Paradigm shift" framing** added to intro — "from stateless inference to stateful reasoning"
2. **"Statefulness in practice" paragraph** added after the 40/40 audit — explicitly says "in a stateless system, all 2,400 entries would be discarded"
3. **Section 4.3 "Architecture Over Model Intelligence"** — already had it (Gemini 0% in other agentic systems → 17.75% via stateful swarms)
4. **New Section 9: "Beyond Legal: The Stateful Swarm Paradigm Is Domain-Agnostic"** — lists applicable domains, emphasizes no domain-specific training needed
5. **Rewritten conclusion** — leads with architecture > model intelligence as the central finding, includes paradigm shift language, memory problem recap, accessibility framing ($1.30 + API key), and the Irys 1,000x claim context

Whitepaper is now 11 pages and covers every insight the README does.

input.prompt:
You are analyzing Datadog's 10-K annual filings from 2020 through 2026 to produce an investment memo about how their strategic priorities have shifted. The filings are PDF files located at:

C:\Users\devan\OneDrive\Desktop\Projects\ant-irys\demo\datadog-strategy\source_documents\

Files:
- 2020-02-25_0001564590-20-006422.pdf (FY2019 10-K)
- 2021-03-01_0001564590-21-009770.pdf (FY2020 10-K)
- 2022-02-25_0001561550-22-000009.pdf (FY2021 10-K)
- 2023-02-24_0001561550-23-000006.pdf (FY2022 10-K)
- 2024-02-23_0001561550-24-000009.pdf (FY2023 10-K)
- 2025-02-20_0001561550-25-000025.pdf (FY2024 10-K)
- 2026-02-18_0001628280-26-008819.pdf (FY2025 10-K)

Read through these filings (focus on the business description, risk factors, MD&A, and financial highlights sections - you can skip the financial statement tables and legal exhibit lists). Then produce a comprehensive investment memo addressing:

1. How has Datadog's product strategy evolved — what new product areas have they entered, which have they emphasized or de-emphasized?
2. How has their go-to-market strategy changed — shifts in customer segments, pricing, land-and-expand dynamics, geographic focus?
3. What risks has the company highlighted as most significant, and how have those risk disclosures changed over time?
4. How has their competitive positioning narrative evolved — who do they see as competitors and how do they differentiate?
5. What does the financial trajectory (revenue growth, margins, R&D spend as % of revenue) reveal about where management is investing?

Be specific — cite exact numbers, quote specific language changes between filings, and note when new sections or disclosures appeared or disappeared. This is a serious investment analysis, not a summary.

Output your complete investment memo as your final response. Make it thorough — this is being compared against an automated swarm system's output.

## Swarm Studio -- Research Summary

### 1. Purpose and Core Functionality

Swarm Studio is an autonomous stateful swarm system designed to replace ChatGPT for "deep work" -- complex research, document analysis, and multi-source investigation. It answers any question by decomposing it into sub-questions, spawning specialized worker agents to research each one, building structured knowledge on a shared blackboard, and iterating until the answer is grounded and complete.

The core thesis: **the blackboard is the first-class primitive, not the agents and not the chat**. Agents are transient; the blackboard persists and compounds intelligence across conversations. Question 11 in a project may be answerable instantly from accumulated state built during questions 1-10.

### 2. Architecture

**Backend:** Python 3.12+, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy (async), PostgreSQL with pgvector for embeddings. Runs via uvicorn. Docker Compose provides a pgvector/pgvector:pg17 database.

**Frontend:** Next.js (TypeScript, React, Tailwind CSS). Dark-themed UI at `localhost:3000` connecting to the backend at `localhost:8000`.

**LLM Layer:** Multi-provider abstraction supporting Gemini (primary), OpenAI, and Anthropic. Two-tier model system:
- **Default tier** (workers, extraction): gemini-3.1-flash-lite / gpt-4.1-mini / claude-haiku-4-5
- **Smart tier** (coordinator decisions, synthesis): gemini-3.5-flash / gpt-4.1 / claude-sonnet-4-6

**Key modules** (all under `src/swarm_studio/`):
- `coordinator/service.py` -- The core orchestration loop (1,545 lines). Handles orientation, decomposition, worker dispatch, reflection, coverage evaluation, and synthesis.
- `blackboard/service.py` -- The 8 blackboard operations (write, read, get, get_many, update_claim_state, supersede, annotate, search).
- `blackboard/policy.py` -- Read-time epistemic policy: computes support status (clean/weak/blocked) by traversing dependency closures.
- `blackboard/coverage.py` -- Structural coverage gate: counts entries per sub-question with source-type breakdown.
- `workers/base.py` -- Worker dispatch: routes to document_reader, web_researcher, analyst, or data_analyst.
- `workers/web_search.py` -- DuckDuckGo search + trafilatura page extraction.
- `workers/document_reader.py` -- PDF (pymupdf), DOCX (python-docx), spreadsheet (pandas), text file reading.
- `workers/data_analysis.py` -- Spreadsheet analysis and chart generation (matplotlib).
- `coordinator/llm.py` -- LLM provider abstraction with real cost tracking from Gemini usage metadata.
- `blackboard/embeddings.py` -- Vector embedding pipeline (text-embedding-004) for semantic search.
- `blackboard/entities.py` -- Automatic entity extraction from entries (LLM-based NER).
- `coordinator/prompts.py` -- All fixed, versioned prompt templates.

**Database schema** (10 tables): projects, chats, entries, entities, entry_entities, annotations, embedding_index, documents, history.

### 3. The Core Algorithm

```
User message
  --> Write instruction entry to blackboard
  --> Orientation call (think about the problem)
  --> If simple: answer directly (1 LLM call, ~$0.003)
  --> If complex: decompose into sub-questions
  --> Spawn workers concurrently (asyncio.gather, 60s timeout)
  --> Extract key findings (contradiction detection)
  --> Auto-generate charts from spreadsheet data
  --> Compute coverage matrix (what's covered, weak, gap)
  --> Reflection call (assess evidence quality, contradictions, gaps)
  --> If sufficient: synthesize final answer
  --> If gaps remain and budget allows: loop (more workers, more rounds)
  --> Final synthesis grounded in cited blackboard entries
```

### 4. Features

**Implemented and functional (based on code + screenshots):**

- **Multi-round research loops** -- The coordinator can run up to 5 rounds of decomposition/research/reflection, each producing more entries.
- **Four worker types** -- document_reader (PDF/DOCX/text/spreadsheet), web_researcher (DuckDuckGo + page crawling + trafilatura), analyst (reasoning over existing entries), data_analyst (quantitative spreadsheet analysis).
- **SSE real-time streaming** -- 13 event types stream to the frontend during a swarm run: thinking, plan, worker_started, key_finding, chart, reflection, round_complete, synthesis, etc.
- **Epistemic trust system** -- Every entry has a claim_state (unverified/verified/stale/disputed) with a formal state machine and authorized-actor enforcement. Dependency-aware reads prevent "epistemic smuggling" (a verified finding citing a disputed source gets blocked).
- **Coverage matrix** -- Structural evaluation of what sub-questions are covered (strong/weak/uncovered) with source-type diversity tracking.
- **Reflection loop** -- After each round, the coordinator critically assesses evidence quality, identifies contradictions and patterns, and suggests follow-up research.
- **Auto-charting** -- Automatically generates matplotlib charts from spreadsheets and PDFs with data extraction, shown inline during streaming.
- **Multi-turn conversation** -- Chat history persists; the coordinator uses it for conversational context.
- **BYOK (Bring Your Own Key)** -- Per-project LLM provider configuration with encrypted key storage.
- **Document upload and management** -- Upload PDF, DOCX, TXT, CSV, XLSX, HTML. Files stored locally with metadata indexes.
- **Working directory support** -- Point a project at a local folder; the coordinator can read files from it.
- **Entity extraction** -- Automatic NER on every source/finding entry, with entity panel in the sidebar.
- **Deduplication** -- Jaccard similarity check (>0.90 threshold) with advisory locks prevents duplicate entries.
- **Epistemic origin classification** -- Each entry is classified by source credibility (primary_extracted, secondary_claim, tertiary_report, model_inference, etc.) using domain lists.
- **Budget-aware planning** -- The coordinator plans within its ceiling and reserves budget for synthesis.
- **User steering** -- Stop button (abort controller) to cancel mid-run.
- **Semantic search** -- pgvector-backed embedding search + keyword fallback.
- **SSRF protection** -- URL validation blocks localhost, private IPs, and metadata endpoints.

**Designed but not yet implemented:**
- Background maintenance system (verification, contradiction discovery, staleness detection, consolidation, promotion) -- fully designed in MAINTENANCE.md but no batch job code exists.
- Blackboard marketplace (pre-built domain blackboards).
- User/org-level scope hierarchy.
- Blackboard inspector panel (the detailed entry-by-entry view).

### 5. Relationship to ant-irys / Stateful Swarm Document Analysis

Swarm Studio is the productized evolution of the ant-irys approach. From DECISIONS.md (D1): the blackboard architecture was "validated by ant-irys (88.5% benchmark with blackboard architecture)." Where ant-irys was a benchmark-targeted system for Harvey LAB (legal document analysis), Swarm Studio generalizes the same blackboard-first, stateful-swarm architecture into a general-purpose product for any research/analysis task.

Key shared concepts:
- Shared blackboard as the coordination primitive (replacing orchestrator-to-worker pair topology)
- Multi-round iterative research with coverage evaluation
- Document + web research in the same swarm run (Mode C)
- The "re-read with new context" capability (Round 3 in ant-irys's document analysis)

Key differences from ant-irys:
- General-purpose (not legal-doc specific)
- Web research capability alongside document analysis
- Persistent project-level knowledge across conversations
- Background maintenance for compounding intelligence
- Full web app UI with streaming reasoning trace
- BYOK multi-provider LLM support
- Usage-based pricing model

### 6. Current State

**Functional prototype with substantial implementation.** Based on the git log (64+ commits), the codebase has been through extensive iteration including 7 Codex review passes. The screenshots show the UI working with real queries (e.g., Tesla vs BYD EV market comparison with 12 concurrent workers). The ServiceNow_Analysis.md is a real output from the system (160 entries researched, 52 web sources, 87 findings, 20 sub-questions).

**What works:** The full loop -- ask a question, watch it decompose, see workers research, get a synthesized answer with citations, cost tracking, multi-turn. Document upload and reading. Web research. Chart generation. BYOK configuration. Project/chat management with delete cascading.

**What's missing for production:** Authentication/authorization, the background maintenance system, production deployment infrastructure, the blackboard inspector UI, user annotation UI (accept/reject/important), and the marketplace. The free/paid tier pricing logic is designed but not implemented in code.

### 7. Notable Capabilities Worth Highlighting

1. **Mode C (document + web simultaneously)** -- The system reads your documents, identifies gaps, searches the web for context, then re-reads the documents with enriched understanding. This re-read-with-new-context loop is the claimed differentiator over ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini which do file OR web sequentially and non-iteratively.

2. **Epistemic trust chain** -- Every piece of knowledge carries a claim_state and provenance. The dependency-aware read policy means a finding can be automatically blocked if its upstream sources become disputed. This is a rare level of rigor for an AI product.

3. **Compounding intelligence** -- The blackboard accumulates knowledge across conversations within a project. The 10th question benefits from the state built by the first 9. The designed maintenance system (not yet built) would actively improve the blackboard while the user is away.

4. **Real-time cost transparency** -- Every query shows its actual cost (tracked from Gemini usage metadata, not estimated). Quick queries can cost $0.003; deep investigations cost $0.50-5.00.

5. **Reflection loop** -- After each research round, the coordinator critically reflects on its own evidence, identifying contradictions, patterns, source diversity issues, and gaps. This self-critique is visible to the user.

6. **Auto-charting from documents** -- The system automatically generates charts from spreadsheet data and even extracts tabular data from PDFs/text for visualization, without the user requesting it.

7. **Sub-cent simple answers** -- Simple questions (direct follow-ups, trivial facts) are answered in a single LLM call without spawning the swarm, at ~$0.003. The system does not over-compute.

### Key File Paths

- Backend entry point: `C:\Users\devan\OneDrive\Desktop\Projects\Swarm Studio\src\swarm_studio\main.py`
- Coordinator (core loop): `C:\Users\devan\OneDrive\Desktop\Projects\Swarm Studio\src\swarm_studio\coordinator\service.py`
- Blackboard service: `C:\Users\devan\OneDrive\Desktop\Projects\Swarm Studio\src\swarm_studio\blackboard\service.py`

- **Epistemic origin classification** -- Each entry is classified by source credibility (primary_extracted, secondary_claim, tertiary_report, model_inference, etc.) using domain lists.
- **Budget-aware planning** -- The coordinator plans within its ceiling and reserves budget for synthesis.
- **User steering** -- Stop button (abort controller) to cancel mid-run.
- **Semantic search** -- pgvector-backed embedding search + keyword fallback.
- **SSRF protection** -- URL validation blocks localhost, private IPs, and metadata endpoints.

**Designed but not yet implemented:**
- Background maintenance system (verification, contradiction discovery, staleness detection, consolidation, promotion) -- fully designed in MAINTENANCE.md but no batch job code exists.
- Blackboard marketplace (pre-built domain blackboards).
- User/org-level scope hierarchy.
- Blackboard inspector panel (the detailed entry-by-entry view).

### 5. Relationship to ant-irys / Stateful Swarm Document Analysis

Swarm Studio is the productized evolution of the ant-irys approach. From DECISIONS.md (D1): the blackboard architecture was "validated by ant-irys (88.5% benchmark with blackboard architecture)." Where ant-irys was a benchmark-targeted system for Harvey LAB (legal document analysis), Swarm Studio generalizes the same blackboard-first, stateful-swarm architecture into a general-purpose product for any research/analysis task.

Key shared concepts:
- Shared blackboard as the coordination primitive (replacing orchestrator-to-worker pair topology)
- Multi-round iterative research with coverage evaluation
- Document + web research in the same swarm run (Mode C)
- The "re-read with new context" capability (Round 3 in ant-irys's document analysis)

Key differences from ant-irys:
- General-purpose (not legal-doc specific)
- Web research capability alongside document analysis
- Persistent project-level knowledge across conversations
- Background maintenance for compounding intelligence
- Full web app UI with streaming reasoning trace
- BYOK multi-provider LLM support
- Usage-based pricing model

### 6. Current State

**Functional prototype with substantial implementation.** Based on the git log (64+ commits), the codebase has been through extensive iteration including 7 Codex review passes. The screenshots show the UI working with real queries (e.g., Tesla vs BYD EV market comparison with 12 concurrent workers). The ServiceNow_Analysis.md is a real output from the system (160 entries researched, 52 web sources, 87 findings, 20 sub-questions).

**What works:** The full loop -- ask a question, watch it decompose, see workers research, get a synthesized answer with citations, cost tracking, multi-turn. Document upload and reading. Web research. Chart generation. BYOK configuration. Project/chat management with delete cascading.

**What's missing for production:** Authentication/authorization, the background maintenance system, production deployment infrastructure, the blackboard inspector UI, user annotation UI (accept/reject/important), and the marketplace. The free/paid tier pricing logic is designed but not implemented in code.

### 7. Notable Capabilities Worth Highlighting

1. **Mode C (document + web simultaneously)** -- The system reads your documents, identifies gaps, searches the web for context, then re-reads the documents with enriched understanding. This re-read-with-new-context loop is the claimed differentiator over ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini which do file OR web sequentially and non-iteratively.

2. **Epistemic trust chain** -- Every piece of knowledge carries a claim_state and provenance. The dependency-aware read policy means a finding can be automatically blocked if its upstream sources become disputed. This is a rare level of rigor for an AI product.

3. **Compounding intelligence** -- The blackboard accumulates knowledge across conversations within a project. The 10th question benefits from the state built by the first 9. The designed maintenance system (not yet built) would actively improve the blackboard while the user is away.

4. **Real-time cost transparency** -- Every query shows its actual cost (tracked from Gemini usage metadata, not estimated). Quick queries can cost $0.003; deep investigations cost $0.50-5.00.

5. **Reflection loop** -- After each research round, the coordinator critically reflects on its own evidence, identifying contradictions, patterns, source diversity issues, and gaps. This self-critique is visible to the user.

6. **Auto-charting from documents** -- The system automatically generates charts from spreadsheet data and even extracts tabular data from PDFs/text for visualization, without the user requesting it.

7. **Sub-cent simple answers** -- Simple questions (direct follow-ups, trivial facts) are answered in a single LLM call without spawning the swarm, at ~$0.003. The system does not over-compute.

import json
bb = json.loads(open('results/datadog-demo/demo/datadog-strategy/swarm/blackboard_iter_12_supervisor_approved_0.json').read())
entries = bb['entries']
types = {}
for e in entries:
    t = e.get('type','?')
    types[t] = types.get(t,0)+1
print(f'Total entries: {len(entries)}')
for t,c in sorted(types.items(), key=lambda x:-x[1]):
    print(f'  {t}: {c}')
sigs = bb.get('signals',[])
open_s = sum(1 for s in sigs if s.get('status')=='open')
addr_s = sum(1 for s in sigs if s.get('status')=='addressed')
exp_s = sum(1 for s in sigs if s.get('status')=='expired')
print(f'Signals: {len(sigs)} total, {addr_s} addressed, {open_s} open, {exp_s} expired')
print(f'Tokens: {bb.get(\"total_tokens_used\",0):,}')
# cost calc
cost_by_model = bb.get('cost_by_model', {})
total_cost = 0
for model, usage in cost_by_model.items():
    inp = usage.get('input', 0)
    out = usage.get('output', 0)
    calls = usage.get('calls', 0)
    if 'flash-lite' in model:
        c = inp * 0.25/1e6 + out * 1.50/1e6
    elif 'flash' in model:
        c = inp * 1.50/1e6 + out * 9.00/1e6
    else:
        c = inp * 0.25/1e6 + out * 1.50/1e6
    total_cost += c
    print(f'{model}: {calls} calls, {inp:,} in / {out:,} out, ~\${c:.2f}')
print(f'Total estimated cost: \${total_cost:.2f}')
"

### Product Strategy (657 words)

### Chronological Evolution of the Platform (2012--2024)

Datadog's product strategy over the past decade highlights a transition from a specialized infrastructure monitoring tool to a comprehensive, unified observability and security platform. The company's expansion has been characterized by a rapid, continuous release cycle designed to capture a larger share of IT spend:

- \*\*2012:\*\* Datadog launched \*\*Infrastructure Monitoring\*\*, establishing its foundational footprint in the IT operations space.

- \*\*2017:\*\* The company expanded vertically into application layers by launching \*\*Application Performance Monitoring (APM)\*\*.

- \*\*2018:\*\* Datadog launched \*\*Log Management\*\*, successfully uniting the \"three pillars of observability\" (metrics, traces, and logs) wit
...

### Competition (507 words)

### Competition and Competitive Positioning

Over the past 5 to 7 years, Datadog's competitive landscape and positioning narrative have evolved from a focus on displacing legacy IT operations management (ITOM) point solutions to establishing a dominant, unified observability and security platform.

#### The 2019 Baseline: Legacy and Point-Solution Competitors

At the time of its initial public offering and in its 2019 Annual Report (Form 10-K), Datadog operated in a highly fragmented market. In 2019, Datadog identified legacy competitors in the IT operations management and application performance monitoring (APM) markets, including:

- \*\*BMC Software\*\*

- \*\*Broadcom\*\* (including CA Technologies)

- \*\*Cisco\*\* (including AppDynamics)

- \*\*Dynatrace\*\*

- \*\*IBM\*\*

- \*\*Mi
...

### Go-to-Market (423 words)

### Go-to-Market Strategy Evolution

Over the past several years, Datadog's go-to-market (GTM) strategy has transitioned from a high-velocity, developer-led, bottom-up acquisition model to a sophisticated, enterprise-grade sales engine designed to capture large-scale cloud migration and digital transformation budgets.

#### The 2019 Baseline: High-Velocity, Inside Sales, and Self-Service

At the time of its initial post-IPO disclosures, Datadog's commercial engine was optimized for rapid customer acquisition with minimal friction.

- \*\*Customer Scale and Footprint:\*\* As of December 31, 2019, Datadog had approximately 10,500 customers spanning over 100 countries (\`\[e2058\]\`). This broad customer base served as the foundational scale for the company\'s highly efficient \"land-and-exp
...

### Financial Performance (749 words)

An analysis of Datadog's financial trajectory over the 2020--2025 period reveals a deliberate transition from a high-growth, land-and-expand model to a highly scaled, profitable platform. By examining revenue growth, operating margins, and the allocation of research and development (R&D) and sales and marketing (S&M) reinvestments, we can discern where management is positioning the company for its next phase of market leadership.

### Early Growth and Aggressive Market Capture (2020)

In fiscal year 2020, Datadog exhibited the classic financial profile of a high-growth SaaS disruptor prioritizing market share and rapid product expansion. \*\*Datadog 2020 revenue was \$603.5 million, a 66.3% YoY growth from 2019 revenue of \$362.8 million\*\* (Form 10-K, Item 6). To fuel this expansion, ma
...

### Operational Metrics (316 words)

An analysis of Datadog, Inc.'s operational metrics from its Form 10-K filed on February 25, 2022, reveals strong customer acquisition momentum, rapid enterprise market penetration, and highly efficient \"land-and-expand\" dynamics.

#### 1. Customer Base Expansion

Datadog has demonstrated a strong capacity to scale its overall customer footprint across diverse market segments:

- \*\*As of December 31, 2020\*\*, Datadog, Inc. had approximately \*\*14,170 customers\*\* (Form 10-K, filed February 25, 2022).

- \*\*As of December 31, 2021\*\*, Datadog, Inc. had approximately \*\*18,800 customers\*\* (Form 10-K, filed February 25, 2022).

This represents a year-over-year growth rate of approximately 32.7%, reflecting robust market demand and effective top-of-funnel customer acquisition.

###
...

### Go-to-Market Strategy (232 words)

Datadog's go-to-market (GTM) strategy has undergone a fundamental structural evolution over the last several years, transitioning from an aggressive sales-led growth engine to a product- and R&D-driven expansion model.

Historically, between 2019 and 2022, Datadog prioritized a sales-led growth strategy, a phase during which Sales and Marketing (S&M) expenses consistently exceeded Research and Development (R&D) expenses. This heavy front-end investment in S&M was designed to rapidly capture market share and establish a broad customer footprint. However, starting in 2023, Datadog executed a strategic pivot toward an R&D-heavy investment model, where R&D spending surpassed S&M spending. This shift underscores a transition toward product-led growth, where continuous feature velocity and plat
...

### Risk Factors (289 words)

An analysis of Datadog's annual filings over the last several years reveals a profound shift in the company\'s risk profile, transitioning from a venture-backed disruptor fighting legacy incumbents to an enterprise platform navigating intense hyperscaler competition and complex global operations.

### Evolution of Competitive Risks

The competitive threat landscape detailed in Datadog\'s filings has fundamentally shifted over the past 5-7 years:

- \*\*Legacy Incumbents (2019):\*\* In 2019, Datadog identified competition from legacy vendors like Cisco, IBM, and CA Technologies as a primary risk. During this period, the company\'s primary challenge was displacing deeply entrenched, on-premise systems management and application performance monitoring (APM) solutions within traditional enter

input.content:
# Domain-Agnostic Proof: Datadog 10-K Strategic Analysis

## The task

Analyze how Datadog's strategic priorities have shifted over the last 5-7 years using their annual 10-K filings (2020-2026). Produce a comprehensive investment memo covering product strategy evolution, go-to-market shifts, risk factor changes, competitive positioning, and financial trajectory.

**Source documents:** 7 Datadog 10-K annual filings (FY2019 through FY2025), totaling ~31MB of PDF.

## irys-stateful-swarms vs. Claude Code

We ran the same task through both systems simultaneously.

### irys-stateful-swarms (stateful swarm)

| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Status | **Completed successfully** |
| Time | 800 seconds (~13 minutes) |
| Tokens used | 2,734,609 |
| Blackboard entries | 2,115 (1,511 observations, 512 calculations, 41 analyses, 41 gaps, 10 strategies) |
| Signals generated | 218 (80 addressed, 55 open, 83 expired) |
| Iterations | 12 |
| Output | 12,657-word investment memo (64KB docx) with 80+ structured sections |
| Models used | Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite (extraction) + Gemini 3.5 Flash (synthesis) |

The system read all 7 filings in parallel, built 2,115 structured findings with source provenance, identified gaps in its own analysis, dispatched targeted follow-up workers, and synthesized a comprehensive investment memo covering corporate structure, product strategy evolution (2012-2024 timeline), competitive positioning shifts, go-to-market transformation, financial performance trajectory, operational metrics, risk factor evolution, geographic expansion, capital structure, and market opportunity.

### Claude Code sub-agent (Claude Opus)

| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Status | **Failed — context window thrashing** |
| Time | 415 seconds (~7 minutes) before failure |
| Output | None |
| Error | "Autocompact is thrashing: the context refilled to the limit within 3 turns of the previous compact, 3 times in a row" |

The sub-agent attempted to read the 7 10-K PDFs but could not hold them in its context window. After 19 tool calls and repeated context compaction cycles, the system gave up. No investment memo was produced.

## Why this happened

This is exactly the problem described in the [white paper](../../whitepaper.pdf), Section 2: "The Memory Problem in Agentic Systems."

**Context compaction destroys information.** The sub-agent tried to read the filings, but each 10-K is hundreds of pages. After reading 2-3 filings, the context window filled up. The system compacted earlier readings to make room for new ones — but then couldn't reference the details it had summarized away. When it tried to read more filings, the cycle repeated until the system recognized it was thrashing and aborted.

**The stateful swarm doesn't have this problem.** Each worker reads a focused section of a specific document and writes structured findings to the blackboard. No single worker needs to hold all 7 filings in context. The blackboard accumulates 2,115 typed, provenance-tracked entries across 12 iterations — none of which are ever compacted or summarized away. When the synthesis phase begins, it works from curated, structured state, not from raw document text.

**This is the fundamental architectural difference.** A stateless system treats the context window as its memory — and context windows are lossy, bounded, and ephemeral. A stateful swarm treats the blackboard as its memory — and blackboards are lossless, unbounded, and persistent.

## What the output looks like

The full output is in [`output.docx`](output.docx). Key sections include:

**Product Strategy Evolution:** Traces Datadog's expansion from infrastructure monitoring (2012) through APM (2017), Log Management (2018), Synthetics and Network Monitoring (2019), Real User Monitoring and Security (2020), to CI Visibility, Database Monitoring, Cloud Cost Management, and AI-native observability products through 2024.

**Competitive Positioning Shifts:** Documents the transition from competing against legacy ITOM vendors (BMC, IBM, CA Technologies, Cisco/AppDynamics) to competing against cloud-native platforms (Elastic, Splunk, Grafana) and hyperscaler-embedded tools (AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, GCP Operations).

**Go-to-Market Transformation:** Tracks the shift from high-velocity inside sales and self-service (10,500 customers in 2019) to enterprise-grade sales motion (29,200+ customers by 2025) with strategic alliances and upmarket penetration ($100K+ ARR customers growing from 858 to 3,810).

**Financial Trajectory:** Revenue from $362.8M (FY2019) to $2.68B+ (FY2025), with the strategic pivot from S&M-heavy spending to R&D-heavy investment starting in 2023 — signaling a shift from sales-led to product-led growth.

**Risk Factor Evolution:** From legacy displacement risks (2019) to hyperscaler competition and complex global operations risks (2025).

## Explore the reasoning trace

Browse the blackboard snapshots to see how the system built its understanding:

- [`swarm/blackboard_iter_0_seed.json`](swarm/blackboard_iter_0_seed.json) — Initial planning: questions generated before reading any documents
- [`swarm/blackboard_iter_6_post_6.json`](swarm/blackboard_iter_6_post_6.json) — Mid-point: extraction in progress across all 7 filings
- [`swarm/blackboard_iter_12_supervisor_approved_0.json`](swarm/blackboard_iter_12_supervisor_approved_0.json) — Final state: 2,115 entries ready for synthesis

## The domain-agnostic point

irys-stateful-swarms was built and benchmarked on legal document analysis (Harvey LAB). **Zero changes were made to run it on SEC filings for investment analysis.** The same swarm coordination, the same blackboard architecture, the same model routing — applied to a completely different domain, producing a comprehensive investment memo that a stateless system couldn't even attempt.

This is what we mean by "the stateful swarm paradigm is domain-agnostic."

| Status | **Completed successfully** |
| Time | 800 seconds (~13 minutes) |
| Tokens used | 2,734,609 |
| Blackboard entries | 2,115 (1,511 observations, 512 calculations, 41 analyses, 41 gaps, 10 strategies) |
| Signals generated | 218 (80 addressed, 55 open, 83 expired) |
| Iterations | 12 |
| Output | 12,657-word investment memo (64KB docx) with 80+ structured sections |
| Models used | Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite (extraction) + Gemini 3.5 Flash (synthesis) |

The system read all 7 filings in parallel, built 2,115 structured findings with source provenance, identified gaps in its own analysis, dispatched targeted follow-up workers, and synthesized a comprehensive investment memo covering corporate structure, product strategy evolution (2012-2024 timeline), competitive positioning shifts, go-to-market transformation, financial performance trajectory, operational metrics, risk factor evolution, geographic expansion, capital structure, and market opportunity.

### Claude Code sub-agent (Claude Opus)

| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Status | **Failed — context window thrashing** |
| Time | 415 seconds (~7 minutes) before failure |
| Output | None |
| Error | "Autocompact is thrashing: the context refilled to the limit within 3 turns of the previous compact, 3 times in a row" |

The sub-agent attempted to read the 7 10-K PDFs but could not hold them in its context window. After 19 tool calls and repeated context compaction cycles, the system gave up. No investment memo was produced.

## Why this happened

This is exactly the problem described in the [white paper](../../whitepaper.pdf), Section 2: "The Memory Problem in Agentic Systems."

**Context compaction destroys information.** The sub-agent tried to read the filings, but each 10-K is hundreds of pages. After reading 2-3 filings, the context window filled up. The system compacted earlier readings to make room for new ones — but then couldn't reference the details it had summarized away. When it tried to read more filings, the cycle repeated until the system recognized it was thrashing and aborted.

**The stateful swarm doesn't have this problem.** Each worker reads a focused section of a specific document and writes structured findings to the blackboard. No single worker needs to hold all 7 filings in context. The blackboard accumulates 2,115 typed, provenance-tracked entries across 12 iterations — none of which are ever compacted or summarized away. When the synthesis phase begins, it works from curated, structured state, not from raw document text.

**This is the fundamental architectural difference.** A stateless system treats the context window as its memory — and context windows are lossy, bounded, and ephemeral. A stateful swarm treats the blackboard as its memory — and blackboards are lossless, unbounded, and persistent.

## What the output looks like

The full output is in [`output.docx`](output.docx). Key sections include:

**Product Strategy Evolution:** Traces Datadog's expansion from infrastructure monitoring (2012) through APM (2017), Log Management (2018), Synthetics and Network Monitoring (2019), Real User Monitoring and Security (2020), to CI Visibility, Database Monitoring, Cloud Cost Management, and AI-native observability products through 2024.

**Competitive Positioning Shifts:** Documents the transition from competing against legacy ITOM vendors (BMC, IBM, CA Technologies, Cisco/AppDynamics) to competing against cloud-native platforms (Elastic, Splunk, Grafana) and hyperscaler-embedded tools (AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, GCP Operations).

**Go-to-Market Transformation:** Tracks the shift from high-velocity inside sales and self-service (10,500 customers in 2019) to enterprise-grade sales motion (29,200+ customers by 2025) with strategic alliances and upmarket penetration ($100K+ ARR customers growing from 858 to 3,810).

**Financial Trajectory:** Revenue from $362.8M (FY2019) to $2.68B+ (FY2025), with the strategic pivot from S&M-heavy spending to R&D-heavy investment starting in 2023 — signaling a shift from sales-led to product-led growth.

**Risk Factor Evolution:** From legacy displacement risks (2019) to hyperscaler competition and complex global operations risks (2025).

## Explore the reasoning trace

Browse the blackboard snapshots to see how the system built its understanding:

- [`swarm/blackboard_iter_0_seed.json`](swarm/blackboard_iter_0_seed.json) — Initial planning: questions generated before reading any documents
- [`swarm/blackboard_iter_6_post_6.json`](swarm/blackboard_iter_6_post_6.json) — Mid-point: extraction in progress across all 7 filings
- [`swarm/blackboard_iter_12_supervisor_approved_0.json`](swarm/blackboard_iter_12_supervisor_approved_0.json) — Final state: 2,115 entries ready for synthesis

## The domain-agnostic point

irys-stateful-swarms was built and benchmarked on legal document analysis (Harvey LAB). **Zero changes were made to run it on SEC filings for investment analysis.** The same swarm coordination, the same blackboard architecture, the same model routing — applied to a completely different domain, producing a comprehensive investment memo that a stateless system couldn't even attempt.

This is what we mean by "the stateful swarm paradigm is domain-agnostic."

file.content:
# Domain-Agnostic Proof: Datadog 10-K Strategic Analysis

## The task

Analyze how Datadog's strategic priorities have shifted over the last 5-7 years using their annual 10-K filings (2020-2026). Produce a comprehensive investment memo covering product strategy evolution, go-to-market shifts, risk factor changes, competitive positioning, and financial trajectory.

**Source documents:** 7 Datadog 10-K annual filings (FY2019 through FY2025), totaling ~31MB of PDF.

## irys-stateful-swarms vs. Claude Code

We ran the same task through both systems simultaneously.

### irys-stateful-swarms (stateful swarm)

| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Status | **Completed successfully** |
| Time | 800 seconds (~13 minutes) |
| Tokens used | 2,734,609 |
| Blackboard entries | 2,115 (1,511 observations, 512 calculations, 41 analyses, 41 gaps, 10 strategies) |
| Signals generated | 218 (80 addressed, 55 open, 83 expired) |
| Iterations | 12 |
| Output | 12,657-word investment memo (64KB docx) with 80+ structured sections |
| Models used | Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite (extraction) + Gemini 3.5 Flash (synthesis) |

The system read all 7 filings in parallel, built 2,115 structured findings with source provenance, identified gaps in its own analysis, dispatched targeted follow-up workers, and synthesized a comprehensive investment memo covering corporate structure, product strategy evolution (2012-2024 timeline), competitive positioning shifts, go-to-market transformation, financial performance trajectory, operational metrics, risk factor evolution, geographic expansion, capital structure, and market opportunity.

### Claude Code sub-agent (Claude Opus)

| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Status | **Failed — context window thrashing** |
| Time | 415 seconds (~7 minutes) before failure |
| Output | None |
| Error | "Autocompact is thrashing: the context refilled to the limit within 3 turns of the previous compact, 3 times in a row" |

The sub-agent attempted to read the 7 10-K PDFs but could not hold them in its context window. After 19 tool calls and repeated context compaction cycles, the system gave up. No investment memo was produced.

## Why this happened

This is exactly the problem described in the [white paper](../../whitepaper.pdf), Section 2: "The Memory Problem in Agentic Systems."

**Context compaction destroys information.** The sub-agent tried to read the filings, but each 10-K is hundreds of pages. After reading 2-3 filings, the context window filled up. The system compacted earlier readings to make room for new ones — but then couldn't reference the details it had summarized away. When it tried to read more filings, the cycle repeated until the system recognized it was thrashing and aborted.

**The stateful swarm doesn't have this problem.** Each worker reads a focused section of a specific document and writes structured findings to the blackboard. No single worker needs to hold all 7 filings in context. The blackboard accumulates 2,115 typed, provenance-tracked entries across 12 iterations — none of which are ever compacted or summarized away. When the synthesis phase begins, it works from curated, structured state, not from raw document text.

**This is the fundamental architectural difference.** A stateless system treats the context window as its memory — and context windows are lossy, bounded, and ephemeral. A stateful swarm treats the blackboard as its memory — and blackboards are lossless, unbounded, and persistent.

## What the output looks like

The full output is in [`output.docx`](output.docx). Key sections include:

**Product Strategy Evolution:** Traces Datadog's expansion from infrastructure monitoring (2012) through APM (2017), Log Management (2018), Synthetics and Network Monitoring (2019), Real User Monitoring and Security (2020), to CI Visibility, Database Monitoring, Cloud Cost Management, and AI-native observability products through 2024.

**Competitive Positioning Shifts:** Documents the transition from competing against legacy ITOM vendors (BMC, IBM, CA Technologies, Cisco/AppDynamics) to competing against cloud-native platforms (Elastic, Splunk, Grafana) and hyperscaler-embedded tools (AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, GCP Operations).

**Go-to-Market Transformation:** Tracks the shift from high-velocity inside sales and self-service (10,500 customers in 2019) to enterprise-grade sales motion (29,200+ customers by 2025) with strategic alliances and upmarket penetration ($100K+ ARR customers growing from 858 to 3,810).

**Financial Trajectory:** Revenue from $362.8M (FY2019) to $2.68B+ (FY2025), with the strategic pivot from S&M-heavy spending to R&D-heavy investment starting in 2023 — signaling a shift from sales-led to product-led growth.

**Risk Factor Evolution:** From legacy displacement risks (2019) to hyperscaler competition and complex global operations risks (2025).

## Explore the reasoning trace

Browse the blackboard snapshots to see how the system built its understanding:

- [`swarm/blackboard_iter_0_seed.json`](swarm/blackboard_iter_0_seed.json) — Initial planning: questions generated before reading any documents
- [`swarm/blackboard_iter_6_post_6.json`](swarm/blackboard_iter_6_post_6.json) — Mid-point: extraction in progress across all 7 filings
- [`swarm/blackboard_iter_12_supervisor_approved_0.json`](swarm/blackboard_iter_12_supervisor_approved_0.json) — Final state: 2,115 entries ready for synthesis

## The domain-agnostic point

irys-stateful-swarms was built and benchmarked on legal document analysis (Harvey LAB). **Zero changes were made to run it on SEC filings for investment analysis.** The same swarm coordination, the same blackboard architecture, the same model routing — applied to a completely different domain, producing a comprehensive investment memo that a stateless system couldn't even attempt.

This is what we mean by "the stateful swarm paradigm is domain-agnostic."

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)
